sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 35, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Player: Two green tokens. Token 6 is at [31, 31] (left of purple bar), Token 7 is at [31, 43] (right of purple bar). - Targets: Two charcoal square outlines. Target 4 is at [28, 45] (right platform), Target 10 is at [34, 13] (left platform). - Environment: Two light gray platforms connected by a bridge, separated by a purple vertical bar (cols 33-38). - Goal: Move the green tokens into the charcoal targets.
- Action model: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT move the tokens. Only one token seems to be active at a time, or they move independently based on some rule. In the last turn, `RIGHT` moved Token 6 but not Token 7.
- Plan: 1. Move Token 6 to Target 10: DOWN 3, LEFT 18. 2. Move Token 7 to Target 4: UP 3, RIGHT 2. 3. Determine how to switch active tokens if only one moves. Let's try moving Token 6 to the left target.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 34 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The user wants me to solve a grid-based puzzle game.
I have two green tokens and two charcoal targets.
One token is on the left platform, one is on the right platform.
The goal is to place both tokens on the targets.
Current state (step 35):
Green tokens:
- Token 6: center [31, 43] (Right platform)
- Token 10: center [37, 16] (Left platform)
Wait, the IDs changed. Let's re-examine.
Previous turn:
- Green Tokens: ID 6: [31, 10], ID 7: [31, 43]
- Targets: ID 4 (c): [28, 45], ID 10 (c): [33, 12]
Current turn (step 35):
- Green Tokens: ID 6: [31, 43], ID 10: [37, 16]
- Targets: ID 4 (c): [28, 45], ID 8 (c): [34, 13]
Wait, the IDs in the segmentation are shifting. I should rely on positions and colors.
Positions:
Token A (Right): [31, 43]
Token B (Left): [37, 16]
Target A (Right): [28, 45]
Target B (Left): [34, 13]
Token A is at [31, 43]. Target A is at [28, 45].
Diff: Row -3, Col +2.
Action sequence: UP, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT.
Token B is at [37, 16]. Target B is at [34, 13].
Diff: Row -3, Col -3.
Action sequence: UP, UP, UP, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
I need to know which token moves when I press the arrow keys.
In previous turns:
- `RIGHT` moved Token 6 (which was at [31, 10]) to [31, 12]... wait.
Let's look at the history.
Step 1 -> 4: `RIGHT` x 3.
Step 4: Token 6 [31, 25], Token 8 [31, 43]. (Originals were [31, 19] and [31, 28])
So both moved? No, 25-19=6, 43-28=15.
Actually, looking at the image, only one token is moving at a time?
No, the image shows both.
Wait, the prompt says "Two green tokens. One at [31, 19] (with a white center) and one at [31, 28] (with a black center)."
Maybe the one with the black center is the active one?
Looking at the image, one token has a black dot in the center, the other has a white dot.
The one with the black dot is the active one.
Current image:
Token on the right [31, 43] has a black dot.
Token on the left [37, 16] has a white dot.
So the right token is active.
To move the right token [31, 43] to target [28, 45]:
UP, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT.
Then I need to switch to the left token. How?
Maybe by moving the right token onto its target? Or by clicking?
Or maybe they switch every X moves?
Let's try moving the right token to its target first.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move the active token (Right) to its target [28, 45]
# Current position: [31, 43]
# Target: [28, 45]
# Diff: Row -3, Col +2
action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
